subject: Tips to outsmart dopamine
body: Why some cancer drugs are in short supply View this email online June 18, 2023by Andrea MuraskinThis week: If you’ve ever struggled to turn off the TV to get your kid or yourself to bed on time, or finished a pint of Haagen Dazs when you planned to stop after a few spoonfuls, these science-based tips on how to outsmart dopamine are for you. Plus: A shortage of some cancer medicines has oncologists and patients worried, and it’s part of a bigger problem with the business model behind generic drugs. And: Dementia can take away our financial awareness – here’s how to plan around it. Before we get into the stories, I just want to give you a heads up that we’ll be taking next week off from the newsletter to recharge our batteries. More about what I’ll be up to at the end of this newsletter. :) Ok, here’s this week’s news! 4 tips to unhook your kid’s – or your own – cravings for sugar and screens Meredith Miotke /for NPRI don’t have a very big following on social media. But one time I wrote an amusing response to a tweet by Peter Sagal, which was followed by 24 hours or so of continuing likes and replies. Each time I refreshed my screen, I experienced a jolt of delight. Yet somehow I never quite felt satisfied, and I was disappointed when the interactions petered out. Those jolts of delight I experienced were my brain producing the neurochemical dopamine. You’ve probably heard that a dopamine surge brings about pleasure, and then is quickly followed by a come-down, which motivates us to want more of whatever brought about the surge – more “likes,” more junk food, more booze, more Succession etc. Dopamine is part of a neural pathway that evolved in our brains to draw us to things that are essential to our survival like water, social interactions and sex. But research about the molecule in the past decades has shown that dopamine does not really bring about pleasure so much as desire. In fact, studies show that over time, people can end up not enjoying the activities that trigger big surges in dopamine – even if they can’t stop doing them. The good news is understanding how the neurochemical works can help us break or cut down on unhealthy habits that drive dopamine spikes. NPR science correspondent Michaeleen Doucleff harnessed neuroscience to help wean her seven year-old off cartoons and cupcakes, with impressive results. Learn Michaeleen’s ‘anti-dopamine parenting’ tips, and strategies to take control over your own cravings. Also: I got 15 mail-order chicks. They ended up changing my life Newsletter continues after sponsor message Some cancer drugs are in short supply, putting patients' care at risk. Here's why Toni DezomitsChemotherapy drugs carboplatin and cisplatin — both core to treating many different cancers — both fell into short supply earlier this year. Denver oncologist Jennifer Rubatt says she fears for her patients. "When I was faced with this drug substitution for a young woman with young kids, I did cry, because if her cancer comes back, I will always question whether it was because I had to give her a substitute," Rubatt says. The shortage of some cancer drugs is part of a larger shortage of generic medicines, which make up over 90% of prescriptions in the U.S. The low price of generic drugs makes it difficult for manufacturers to turn a profit, says Stanford professor Dr. Kevin Schulman. Global inflation and the COVID-19 pandemic accelerated factory shutdowns. Observers say pharmaceutical companies and healthcare systems need a new business model to ensure a good quality, steady supply. Read more about how patients with cancer and their doctors are coping with the shortages. Plus: When to consider experimental cancer therapies, and when not to A loved one's dementia will break your heart. Don't let it wreck your finances Katie Blackley/WESASharon Gwinn was shocked when she found out her husband racked up a $3,000 bill buying rounds for strangers at a bar. It turned out he had been spending money erratically in other ways too. It was all totally out of character. Later, she learned that her husband's loss of financial awareness was the first of a series of cognitive changes that led to an eventual dementia diagnosis. As NPR has reported, a study from Johns Hopkins University found that people with Alzheimer's and related dementias started to develop subprime credit up to six years before getting diagnosed. WESA reporter Sarah Boden asked financial advisers and mental health professionals for advice on how we can protect ourselves and our loved ones’ finances as we age. They recommend putting guardrails in place, such as working with companies that keep track of bills and monitor for fraud and unusual spending; and developing a plan around money management with family members, even when that conversation is uncomfortable. Find out how to be better safe than sorry when it comes to dementia and money. Also: As some families learn the hard way, dementia can take a toll on financial health Before you go: Meredith Rizzo These kids turned their concrete schoolyard into a park, and it could be a model to make cities healthier Testosterone is probably safe for your heart. But it can't stop 'manopause' Are masks for the birds? Answers to reader queries about this new stage of the pandemic New research shows indoor pollution from gas stoves is worse than we thoughtAs a reminder, there will be no NPR Health newsletter on June 25th. I'll be heading to WBUR’s Cityspace in Boston for a live recording of the podcast Vibe Check on Thursday. I can’t wait to catch up with former host of NPR’s It’s Been a Minute Sam Sanders, and ask co-host Zach Stafford how his “year of maybe” is going. While we’re away, check out Sam’s advice on how to have a good weekend. And as always, you'll be able to find NPR's health journalism on Shots. See you in July! Andrea Muraskin and your NPR Health editors Listen to your local NPR station. Visit NPR.org to hear live radio from KUNC (edit station). What do you think of today's email? We'd love to hear your thoughts, questions and feedback: shots@npr.org Enjoying this newsletter? Forward to a friend! They can sign up here. Looking for more great content?Check out all of our newsletter offerings — including Goats & Soda, Daily News, Best of NPR and more! subject: ‘Modest Rainstorm’
body: Forbes NewslettersPlus: Introducing The 2023 30 Under 30 Asia ADVERTISEMENTBECOME A MEMBER • Newsletters • MyForbesGood morning, Nintendo released its much-anticipated installment to the Legend of Zelda franchise, Tears of the Kingdom, less than a week ago—and the video gaming giant announced that it sold over 10 million copies in its first three days. It’s flirting with top all-time sales records, particularly noteworthy given the game only released on a single console: Nintendo’s Switch.Critics, reviewers and yours truly have been mesmerized by the game, with many already calling it a strong contender for Game of the Year. If you’re one of the more than 10 million, good luck out there in Hyrule, Sarah Whitmire Senior Editor, Newsletters Follow me on Twitter and Forbes.comBREAKING NEWSMontana Gov. Greg Gianforte AP/Bobby Caina Calvan, File Montana became the first state in the U.S. to ban TikTok yesterday, implementing a fine for app stores that offer it starting January 1. It’s unclear how the ban will be enforced, though it will not punish or fine individual TikTok users, and it’s likely to face legal challenges. Disgraced Theranos CEO Elizabeth Holmes will report to prison at the end of the month for her 11-year sentence for defrauding investors, after her request to stay out of prison while appealing her conviction was denied. Holmes will likely begin her sentence at a minimum-security prison in Bryan, Texas.BUSINESS + FINANCEMike Roffler, former CEO of First Republic AFP VIA GETTY IMAGESIn opening remarks to House lawmakers on Wednesday, former First Republic CEO Mike Roffler blamed other failed banks for “contaminating” his firm, along with the media, social media and “technological advancements” around digital banking. Rep. Brad Sherman (D-Calif.) called First Republic one of the “three worst-run banks in America,” saying it got knocked out by a “modest rainstorm.”Growing optimism in the tech industry, powered by artificial intelligence, drove gains on Wall Street yesterday. The Dow jumped more than 400 points, and seven tech heavyweights, led by Tesla, Nvidia and Amazon, tacked on a combined $123 billion in market cap. TECH + INNOVATIONThe manufacturing process behind semiconductors, a necessary component of computer chips, is messy from the environmental perspective—a typical plant can produce 10 million gallons of polluted wastewater daily. Water-tech startup Gradient said it reached a $1 billion valuation yesterday, and it expects revenue to double this year to nearly $200 million, signaling the fast-growing need for cleaning up and recycling industrial wastewater. The Securities and Exchange Commission could soon bring enforcement action against Coinbase, and it is likely to mirror moves the agency brought against rival crypto exchanges Bittrex and Kraken, Berenberg analysts said in a research report. WEALTH + ENTREPRENEURSHIPA judge ruled that prosecutors in the U.S. Virgin Islands can subpoena billionaire Elon Musk through Tesla as part of their civil lawsuit against JPMorgan Chase over the bank’s ties to Jeffrey Epstein. Attorneys had tried to subpoena Musk since April for communication records he has with Epstein—they believe Epstein “may have referred or attempted to refer (Musk) to JPMorgan.” MONEY + POLITICSDemocrats are openly raising concerns about President Joe Biden’s willingness to compromise on work requirements for welfare recipients as part of ongoing negotiations to raise the federal debt limit. Rep. Jared Golden (D-Maine) criticized the president for failing to negotiate with Republicans earlier, joining a growing coalition of progressives who are critical of stricter welfare requirements. TRENDS + EXPLAINERSGETTY IMAGESWork from home and other benefits may be at risk after the emergency declaration for Covid-19 expired last week—and it comes with some tax consequences. The dilemma for employers: cease Covid-19 related payments, accept that they might be taxable or see if they might be otherwise deductible. For workers, perks like home internet stipends and WFH computers hang in the balance. Daily Cover Story Meet The Forbes 30 Under 30 Asia Class Of 2023READ ARTICLETOPLINEWith global economic uncertainty on the rise and a significant drop in VC funding, young entrepreneurs in Asia-Pacific have faced a new set of challenges in the past year. But the current environment hasn’t killed innovation. Judging by this year’s cohort of 30 Under 30 Asia honorees, creativity has proven to be a valuable asset to survive—and thrive—amid tougher circumstances.With a total of $1.6 billion in VC funding raised by this year’s listees (compared to $3 billion last year), their valuations may not be as high but their impact is: from deploying AI to streamline tasks across industries to tackling financial literacy inclusion and revamping craftsmanship-based and adaptive fashion. These outstanding young individuals are driven by their determination and desire for positive change.WHY IT MATTERSTake the duo of Akshay Rampuria and Yashovardhan Poddar, who founded Openhouse in 2018: they saw limitations in the Indian education system, and launched what became a chain of after-school tutoring hubs that offer support in math and science, as well as extracurriculars like robotics and hip-hop dance. Openhouse raised $17 million on the idea that “offline” education—aided by technology but taking place in-person—is a more holistic approach to learning.MOREMeet The Young Female Investors On The 30 Under 30 Asia 2023 ListADVERTISEMENTFACTS AND COMMENTSThe World Meteorological Organization released its latest predictions on climate change, predicting humanity will cross the key threshold of 1.5C by 2027: 1.5CExperts believe global temperatures below this threshold can help avoid the worst impacts of climate change. 98%Chance that one of the next five years will be the hottest on record. ‘Uncharted territory’Health, food and water security, as well as the environment, will all be impacted, WHO warns.STRATEGY AND SUCCESSThe word “diverse” is often used as a shortcut when speaking about diversity, equity and inclusion, but the word has its limits—and DEI is a space where words matter. Diverse is an adjective that describes a group or community, but it is not descriptive of an individual. This piece offers a helpful rundown on DEI literacy. This is a feature that has been considered in the past, and your email reinforces the need for it. We have added it to our feature request list and will prioritize it based on customer demand and feasibility.

While we cannot provide a definite timeline for implementation, we will keep you updated on any developments regarding this feature.

In the meantime, please let us know if you have any other questions or suggestions.

Sincerely,
Aetheros Support Team

subject: [spambayes-dev] PING?
body: on Sun Jul 29 2007, David Abrahams wrote: > My analysis is as follows: HammieFilter.close (in sb_filter.py) jumps through > all sorts of hoops to remember the mode in which the DB was opened, and > avoid calling store() if it was only opened for read. > > def close(self): > if self.h is not None: > if self.mode != 'r': > self.h.store() > self.h.close() > self.h = None > > So it sorta looks like Tony's patch was applied. > > However, Hammie.close (in hammie.py) just barrels ahead and calls > store() unconditionally... > > I'm not sure what the right fix here would be. Keep HammieFilter from > calling Hammie.close() when the DB was not opened for write? Sink the > close/store/mode-checking logic from HammieFilter into Hammie itself? > Something else? I'm happy to submit a patch, but I want to make sure it makes sense. Can I get some guidance about the intended approach, please? Thanks, -- Dave Abrahams Boost Consulting http://www.boost-consulting.com The Astoria Seminar ==> http://www.astoriaseminar.com _______________________________________________ spambayes-dev mailing list bsfegmjrr-hnq@python.org http://mail.python.org/mailman/listinfo/spambayes-dev

subject: ⌛ 2023 is already half over
body: Plus, save on your grocery bill this month View this email online Connie Hanzhang Jin/NPRHAPPY HALF NEW YEAR! That’s right, we’re officially at 2023’s midway point. Instead of celebrating with champagne and sequins, it’s time to check in on the New Year’s resolutions you set six months ago. If that makes you a tad anxious…same, bestie. As is my custom, most of my New Year’s resolutions have not survived the spring. My Duolingo streak has evaporated, my sleep is still dysregulated and my savings account is less flush than I would like. Alas. Expecting instant — and significant — behavior shifts from ourselves is usually an exercise in futility. But here’s the thing: I love resolutions, and I’ll probably never stop making them. It gives me a chance to reflect on and affirm my values. Even if I won’t be hitting a 365-day Duolingo streak anytime soon (perdóname, Duo), I still care about learning Spanish. I’ve been watching The Heist on Netflix and practicing speaking with my sisters when I can. I’m not reaching my goal of eight or more hours of sleep each night, but I’m still trying. Lately I’ve been reading before bed instead of scrolling Instagram, and I like to think it helps. If I frame resolutions as part of my self-growth, I'm allowed to pivot. And halfway through the year is a great time to recommit to those goals — or pick new ones entirely. For example, perfecting my sunscreen routine has moved up my priority list. To help you take stock of your year and – if you feel up to it – your life, check out Life Kit’s Resolution Planner. What are your values, and how do they translate to habits? This page has more than 40 guides to help you think through that question. Consider working on wasting less food, training your dog or finding a mentor — and don’t forget to give yourself a break.
